does: This paper introduces the first practical adversarial attack against LLM pruning methods, showing that attackers can construct poisoned models that appear benign in their full form but exhibit malicious behavior after pruning. The core method leverages a proxy metric to estimate which parameters will survive pruning, allowing an adversary to inject harmful behaviors into those parameters while masking the attack in the full model using parameters targeted for removal. The authors develop techniques to compute these pruning likelihood estimates and demonstrate successful attacks on popular pruning methods used in inference engines like vLLM, effectively creating trojan-like vulnerabilities that activate specifically when models undergo standard compression.
connects: This work extends recent research on adversarial attacks against neural network compression techniques and model security. It builds on prior work examining vulnerabilities in knowledge distillation and quantization methods, but focuses specifically on pruning—a less-studied compression approach. The attack relates to broader research on trojan insertion and backdoor attacks in deep learning, but adapts these concepts to the pruning setting by exploiting the deterministic nature of modern pruning algorithms. It also connects to work on interpreting and understanding which parameters are critical for model behavior, leveraging insights from network analysis and parameter importance estimation methods used in legitimate pruning research.
enables: This research opens new directions for security-aware pruning method design and auditing compressed LLMs. It motivates the development of robust pruning techniques that are resistant to such poisoning attacks, potentially requiring randomization or adversarial training approaches. The work suggests a broader security consideration: inference-stage optimizations like pruning, quantization, and other deployment-time modifications may introduce new attack surfaces. Future research could explore detection methods for poisoned models, design provably-safe pruning strategies, and examine similar vulnerabilities in other compression techniques. This also highlights the need for security testing of open-source models before deployment and stronger verification mechanisms for model integrity in supply chains.
